ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar May-29-2026 Continued To Aug-04-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar May-07-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: SPECIALLY APPEARING DEFENDANT KENNY WONG'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS (PURPORTEDLY SERVED ON MARCH 17, 2026)

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: The motion to quash service of summons is denied.

Specially appearing defendant Kenny Wong contends that he was not properly personally served at the Hall of Justice on March 17, 2026. He challenges the filed proof of service of summons (Ex. A to the Wong Declaration) by providing a declaration that his name was spoken and papers were dropped; he also provides a declaration from a witness. Plaintiff provides a declaration from the process server attesting to hand-to-hand service.

"When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process, the burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 [internal quotation marks, citation, and footnote omitted].) The declaration of a process server establishes a presumption affecting the burden of producing evidence that service was properly made. (Evid. Code, sec. 647.)

Wong has met his initial burden of producing evidence to overcome the filed proof of service. The court ultimately finds, however, that plaintiff has met his burden of showing that service was properly made. The court credits the declaration of registered process server Ian Martin because it is specific and descriptive, and because his status as a registered process server provides him with strong incentive to be truthful and accurate in declarations to the court.

Even if the court credited Wong's declarations, it would nonetheless deny the motion. Actual notice may defeat a motion to quash when there is substantial compliance with the service statute. (Ramos v. Homeward Residential, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1434, 1443-1444.) Here, Wong has actual notice and the court finds substantial compliance.

Proposed order is submitted for review. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)

Motion For Reconsideration Order Denying Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
Off Calendar Feb-17-2026 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Hearing held. ARGUED. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT KENNY WONG's Motion For Reconsideration Order Denying Motion To Quash Service Of Summons.

The motion for reconsideration is denied.

A reconsideration motion is governed by Code of Civil Procedure, Sec. 1008, subdivision (a), which states in relevant part that, "[w]hen an application for an order has been made to a judge, or to a court, and refused in whole or in part, or granted, or granted conditionally, or on terms, any party affected by the order may, within 10 days after service upon the party of written notice of entry of the order and based upon new or different facts, circumstances, or law, make application to the same judge or court that made the order, to reconsider the matter and modify, amend, or revoke the prior order." Defendant Kenny Wong's motion does not present any new or different facts, circumstances, or law.

Wong also seeks relief under Code of Civil Procedure, Sec. 473, subdivision (b), which states that "[t]he court may, upon any terms as may be just, relieve a party or the party's legal representative from a judgment, dismissal, order, or other proceeding taken against the party through the party's mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect." Wong cites surprise or mistake in his failure to contest the tentative ruling but offers no basis why the court's original decision was incorrect or based on any mistake of law or fact.

The court denies sanctions to plaintiffs.

Proposed order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Nov-20-2025 Continued To Feb-03-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Nov-12-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KENNY WONG MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS

Defendant Kenny Wong's motion to quash service of summons is DENIED.

"When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process, the burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 [internal quotation marks, citation, and footnote omitted].) Plaintiffs Li Chang Kuang and Tommy Chan presented adequate evidence in their counsel's declaration to carry their burden that effective service was made.

Defendant Wong's argument that Defendant Park Avenue Banning, Inc. was not properly served is moot. Only Defendant Kenny Wong is opposing this motion; there is no indication that Defendant Park Avenue Banning, Inc.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Visiting Judge: Dennis Hayashi; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/DH)
